chapters.title: Writing as a path through old wounds
chapters.text:
Javier Zamora had to grow up way before his time. As a small boy, he was torn from his parents and spent years apart from them. To get back into their arms, he had to face trials no child should ever know.

El Salvador was tearing itself apart in 1990, the year Javier was born. Poverty, violence, and political witch hunts were part of daily life. His father crossed into the United States when Javier was just a year old. Four years later, his mother did the same, leaving him with his grandparents. The deal was that they would save up for his trip and bring him over as soon as they could.

Nothing could have prepared him for what that trip turned out to be. It lasted nine weeks, and for seven of those weeks, his family was in the dark about whether he was even alive.

He was only nine when he made the three-thousand-mile trek through the Sonoran Desert to the Arizona border. Years later, as a grown man in his thirties, he spent long years in therapy to pull the memory back together and tell it the way a scared nine-year-old would. His memoir is about exile, growing up, the ache of missing someone, the taste of fear, the quiet strength of faith, and the wild resilience of a child who had no choice but to keep going.

Putting a wound into words is one way the body starts to let it go.

In this summary, Javier narrates in the voice of his nine-year-old self. Walk with this quiet, stubborn traveler as he peels back his chest and lets you see the journey from the inside. You may come out the other side a little different from when you started.

chapters.title: No one can take a parent place
chapters.text:
Javier grows more and more excited as the trip approaches. His parents say the word "journey" to him over and over on the phone. Legal ways of getting a US visa have failed, so he will have to cross the border the same way his parents did. Javier dreams of seeing them soon and makes that wish every night before bed. He knows several people from his village who once tried the same trip, and none of them came back.

He remembers his mother saying goodbye at dawn. He was five. She kissed him while he was half-asleep and promised she would be back for him soon. He still wishes he had been awake to see her off. All he saw was her shadow in the doorway before he fell back asleep. In the morning, he woke up to an empty room and realized she was gone.

Javier tries hard to remember his father, but the image is hazy. He only knows one story about him. His father did not say goodbye. He just picked up a backpack one night and started walking down the road out of town. One-year-old Javier, who had just learned to walk, saw this and toddled after him. In the morning, the whole family panicked and went looking for the baby, afraid his father had taken him. They found Javier sitting alone on the road.

Being separated from parents at a young age leaves a deep longing in a child.

Nine-year-old Javier tries to convince his parents he is old enough to travel. He can already take care of himself and climb fences with ease, but he is worried that he still cannot tie his shoelaces. He works hard at school because he wants his parents to be proud of him.

In fifth grade, the day he has been waiting for finally arrives. He lies to his teachers, saying he is going to Guatemala with his grandfather and will miss a week of class. He cannot tell the truth, because there are rumors that teachers report students who plan to cross the border illegally. He is proud that no one caught him in the lie, because it means he is one step closer to his parents. The journey begins.

chapters.title: Growing up means learning to be alone
chapters.text:
Javier cannot break the news to his friends, so he hands them his favorite toys as quiet goodbyes. He crams a backpack with the bare necessities and pulls on dark clothes, the way the older travelers told him to.

He is thrilled to think of seeing his parents again, but it means leaving the grandparents who raised him. His grandmother weeps as she holds him one last time. He cannot understand why she is so broken up, because he fully expects to come back for a visit. She is afraid they will never lay eyes on each other again.

His grandfather rides with him as far as Guatemala, where he hands him over to a coyote, a man who guides people across the border for cash. The coyote introduces Javier to the rest of the group. Among them is a woman called Patricia, the same first name as his mother. Her daughter Carla is a couple of years older than Javier, and he warms to her right away.

His grandfather tells him to stick close to the adults at all times. For the first week, he walks next to his grandson, and they grow close in a way they never were. Javier used to fear his grandfather, especially when he drank too much and got mean. This trip showed him a softer side. They talk more in a week than in all of Javier's nine years put together.

Even so, his grandfather has to leave him soon. The thought fills Javier with dread, but his parents soothe him on the phone and promise he will be with them in just a few days. He cannot hold back the tears when his grandfather finally turns and walks away. From this point on, he is on his own.

Each of us has to walk our own road, but the strangers we pass on the way can make the road feel less lonely.

Javier has to drill new lies into his head. He memorizes a fake name and a fake birthday. He has to pass as Mexican and know the anthem and a few key facts about the country's past. The group will cross into Mexico by boat.

By the way, "solito" is a Spanish word that does not translate neatly. It means "on its own," but it also carries the picture of a small bird leaving the nest for the very first time.

chapters.title: Kindness and care make the hardest tests bearable
chapters.text:
The boats carry the migrants for roughly sixteen hours. Javier cowers from the dark and the rolling waves. The people around him are sick from the motion, and he prays for land. He wants to spot a whale in the water, because his grandmother told him whales bring good luck.

Patricia treats him like her own son. She wraps her arms around him and Carla together to keep the two kids warm. Another traveler, Chino, also looks out for Javier. He tells the boy that he reminds him of his little brother back home. Chino gathers Javier into his arms and hums him to sleep.

Javier has no way of knowing yet that these three near-strangers are about to become his second family. When the boat finally reaches Mexico, the coyote hands out fresh fake papers. He says it is safer if the group pretends to be one family.

From there, the group has to pass bus checkpoints and run into the Mexican police. Javier keeps his eyes shut and pretends to sleep, but he hears the soldiers order three men off the bus. The soldiers push them to their knees and start asking questions. Then they drag Javier and his fake family off too. The officers call them criminals and order them flat on the ground, guns pointed at their heads. These cops are on the take and only want a payoff.

The coyote finally steps forward with cash. The officers shrug it off as a mistake and let the group go. As they leave, the cops grab other men who cannot pay. No one knows what happens to them.

"Migrantes" is the word the locals use for us. The word sits in my throat like seawater.

The group follows the train tracks on foot after the bus pulls away without them. They are drained and scared, but also grateful to still be breathing. Their next destination is the city of Acapulco. Javier can see the trip has gone off the rails, because they were never supposed to be here. Even with the fear and the tiredness, he holds on to a thread of adventure and uses his imagination to steady himself in the worst moments.

Standing up to fear and walking through it leaves a feeling nothing else can match.

chapters.title: You grow the most in the hardest stretches
chapters.text:
Javier thinks a lot about his fake family and starts to notice the ways Patricia reminds him of his mother. He also believes he is in love with Carla. Chino pretends to be his father, even though he is only nineteen, and he does things that Javier's real father never did. Javier dreams of introducing his fake family to his parents, but he is worried that his mother will be jealous of Patricia. He hopes they will all live next door to each other in the US and that he and Carla will go to the same school.

Through all the travel and hardship, Javier keeps reflecting. In his rich imagination, you can already see the poet he will become. He tries to look strong and grown-up, even when he is sad and lonely. There is something heartbreaking about the bravery of a child this far from home.

Their next stop is Guadalajara. The migrants have to pretend to be locals again. They stay in a small house for more than ten days, and another delay makes everyone tense. The coyote tells them they need to rest before the hardest part, the desert crossing. He forbids the group from leaving the house, opening windows, or talking to anyone. Locked in the same room, they all miss the people waiting for them in the US. They fear they will never get there. The plans keep changing.

The dread of what is coming can wear you out more than the hardship itself.

The coyote reports that the group will have to walk fifteen kilometers through the desert at night. Everyone is scared. Patricia tells them to pray harder than they ever have. Javier eats a big meal to build his strength, because he will have to jump fences. They will need to walk for eight to ten hours in total silence and stay out of sight. The migration service has helicopters, binoculars, dogs, and guns, so the group cannot get caught. This is the last, hardest, most decisive push before the United States.

In the desert, they meet other groups of migrants from different countries. Javier sees them all as one big family. In his mind, bats and lizards are his pets, the desert is his home, and he lives a small inner life there.

chapters.title: The road teaches you as you walk it
chapters.text:
It takes three tries to cross the desert.

The first attempt ends badly. The group gets past several barbed wire fences, and Javier treats it like an obstacle game. The coyote whistles when an immigration helicopter flies over them, and everyone drops to the ground and tries to blend into the sand. Javier wishes they had weapons to fight back.

The migrants make it through the desert that first time. Now they have to wait for vans to take them across the border. They are one step from the finish line, and the group cheers. But exhaustion wins, and they fall asleep.

A siren, gunshots, screaming, and barking dogs wake them up. Chino is strong and fast enough to run, but he stops to try to save his fake family. It does not work. The officers take them to the police station, where they lock Patricia and Carla in one cell and Chino and Javier in another. The world has come apart.

They are trapped. The worst part for Javier is the fear of being left alone. The soldiers keep them behind bars for a full day, threatening the group with a ten-year prison sentence if they break the law again. The police deport the migrants back to Mexico. The moment they are free, they start planning a second attempt.

They have to do it all over again, and this time it is even scarier. They face the same fences, hear the same whistles, and move in the same silence. They are so thirsty that they risk sneaking up to a fence where there is a tap with water.

They barely have time to drink before a shout and a soldier appear. The police catch them again. This time, the officer is merciful and does not lock them up.

Patricia says it is because the officer is one of them. He releases them, but he sends them back to the same starting point. They are not done yet.

Brave and stubborn people always find a way.

The third try works. The fake family crosses the desert, reaches the vans waiting for them at the border, and crosses into the United States.

Our shadows are so small, but they touch. We are one big shadow. Our own family.

chapters.title: Final thoughts and what to read next
chapters.text:
Javier never got the chance to bring his fake family home to meet his real one. Patricia, Carla, and Chino headed for Virginia, while his parents carried him to California. The connection faded within months.

The bright spot is that he did see his grandparents again years later.

Two decades after the journey, Javier put the whole story down on paper for the world to read. It is a wrenching account of how a child slowly grows into himself while buried in a chain of borrowed names. It is also the story of how, after losing touch with blood relatives, he stumbles into a new kind of family made up of strangers whose only compass is unearned kindness and care.

Javier still hopes he will cross paths with his fake family one day. He regrets that he never properly thanked them for what they did. His mother calls them his guardian angels, but he knows better. They were flesh and blood human beings. They were scared and hurting, and they still found the heart to be kind and tender.

Javier also sets the book down in memory of the people who did not make it. He watched officers drag people out of cars at roadblocks, saw some get locked behind bars, and knew that others wandered off into the desert and likely never came out. Children and pregnant women were among them. He prays for each of them, even though he never learned a single name or saw a single face. He only saw the shapes of people moving through the silence of the night.

Try this next.

Read Javier Zamora's poetry book "Unaccompanied," which is about the strange, lonely life of a child refugee.

Watch the drama El Norte, made in 1983 by Gregory Nava and based on a true story. El Norte was the first Latin American independent film to earn an Academy Award nomination for Best Original Screenplay.

Dig into the wider culture of El Salvador, which has given the world writers like Francisco Gavidia, poets like Roque Dalton, film directors like Patricia Chica, and visual artists like Fernando Llort.

If you made it to the end, thank you for reading.
